content:
7 min read
Just now

--

Life eats “distinctions” to stay alive.
To maintain the few, critical distinctions that define its very existence, life disposes of a vast amount of microscopic distinctions into the environment in a way that can never be recovered.
Intelligence, then, is a compression mechanism that selectively extracts only the distinctions necessary for predicting the future and taking action. In other words, intelligence edits “distinctions” to anticipate what lies ahead.
And AI? Perhaps it’s the beginning of this very mechanism of compression and prediction being externalized outside the human body, starting to take on a limited form of autonomy.
If you’re wondering what on earth I’m talking about, I don’t blame you.
It might sound a bit cryptic at first.
But if you’d like to join me in a little philosophical thought experiment — one that connects three different maps of reality: a worldview inspired by quantum information theory, non-equilibrium thermodynamics, and evolutionary theory — what I mean might just start to click.
If you’re curious, read on.
You might just walk away seeing the world in a whole new light.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The “Interface” for Understanding the World
In my previous post, “What If Reality Is Just a User Interface?”, I aised a rather provocative question:
Modern physics is quietly dissolving “objects” and even “space” itself.
Evolutionary theory suggests our perceptions were never meant to reveal “truth.”
Together, “reality” might just be a “user interface (effective description)” designed for a living organism’s survival through evolution.
The core of this “quantum informational worldview” comes down to two main points:

• Out of the underlying quantum descriptions, states that appear classically stable are selected through interactions with the environment and quantum decoherence.

• Living organisms like us, equipped with perception and cognition, go a step further by selectively compressing this world into a realm of “objects,” “symbols,” and “meanings” suited for survival and action.
This got me thinking.
If that’s the case, then shouldn’t concepts like temperature, entropy, non-equilibrium, and dissipation — and even the very unit we call a living organism — also be regarded as “effective concepts”?
Meaning, they aren’t direct copies of an ultimate reality, but rather ideas that depend entirely on a specific scale and observability.
Could some of these even be interface concepts designed to help us understand quantum informational processes at a classical scale?
It seems highly plausible that these concepts only hold true when we carve up the entire quantum world based on “where we draw the boundary of the system,” “which observables we can access,” and “at what spatiotemporal resolution we are looking.”
In fact, some operational studies suggest that the division of a quantum system into subsystems is determined by the interactions and measurements available to us.
Therefore, the description of dissipation in an open system depends heavily on how we separate the system from the environment and which observables we choose to track.
Note: I’m using the word “interface” on two levels here. One is the physical, operational interface that defines subsystems, observables, and coarse-graining. The other is the perceptual interface through which life experiences the world as objects, symbols, and meanings. While they aren’t identical, they share a common thread: they leave behind only the usable distinctions through limited interactions.
Now, let’s try combining this argument with the idea of the “hierarchical advancement of dissipative mechanisms” that I introduced in “Is Humanity a Hydrothermal Vent for Intelligence?” — the journey of life leaping out of deep-sea vents, and AI now seemingly ready to leap out of the human body.
Redefining Entropy and Dissipative Structures
First, let’s try connecting entropy with quantum information theory by reframing entropy production as the “disappearance of distinctions.”
By “distinction,” I mean a difference that can manifest as a variance in physical interaction, measurement, or response.
If you look into open quantum systems that satisfy certain conditions — such as quantum Markov processes or quantum dynamical semigroups with steady states — a standard formulation shows that the entropy production rate can be expressed as the speed at which the quantum relative entropy between the current state and a reference state decreases.
In plain English?
The entropy production rate is a measure of how fast the “distinctions” that were recoverable in that effective description are being transferred to the environment (or to the correlation between the system and the environment), making them unrecoverable.
Now, let’s bring in the perspective of “dissipative structures.”
In standard dissipative structure theory, we usually think of it like this:
An energy gradient exists, and orderly structures are formed in the process of dissipating that energy.
But if we flip this view based on our quantum informational approach, it looks like this:
We perceive the changes in “distinguishability” and the “rearrangement of correlations” happening within the fundamental informational structure as energy gradients, heat, entropy production, and dissipative structures.
In other words, dissipative structures aren’t fundamental building blocks of the universe. Instead, they might just be how certain aspects of a deeper, underlying quantum informational process manifest to our human perception.
From this viewpoint, a dissipative structure is a dynamic framework that sustains a macro-pattern distinguishable from its background through the flow of matter and energy.
To put it even more boldly: it might just be a shadow cast by a fundamental informational process onto our classical interface.
If we look at the evolutionary lineage I discussed in the hydrothermal vent article — Convection → Chemical Reaction → Biological Intellect → Civilization → AI — this isn’t just a story of “advancing energy dissipation capabilities.” Instead, we can re-interpret it as a hierarchical evolution of informational patterns:

• In physical structures, stable distinctions happen to remain as a result of physics.

• In life, distinctions that help with self-preservation are actively maintained.

• In intelligence, distinctions are learned, predicted, and edited.
Re-reading “Evolution” Through the Lens of Information
Through this lens, the evolutionary lineage of advancing dissipative mechanisms can be beautifully reinterpreted:

• Physical Structures: Convection currents or vortices maintain a certain pattern under an energy gradient.
Informationally speaking, they are distinguishable patterns that survive relatively long amidst environmental fluctuations.

• Life: Living things actively maintain their own boundaries and internal states.
They aren’t just passive patterns that happen to exist; they are patterns that actively preserve their own distinguishability.

• Intelligence: Intellect doesn’t process the entirety of reality. Instead, it predictively extracts only the distinctions critical for survival.
This means intelligence isn’t a device that perfectly mirrors the absolute truth of the world; it’s a compression mechanism that decides which distinctions to keep and which to ignore to sustain its future self.

• Civilization: Through writing, currency, laws, science, cities, and networks, civilization stores distinctions outside the individual biological body.
The development of civilization is an expansion of energy consumption, but it’s equally the development of an interface that externalizes memory, classification, prediction, and control.

• AI: We can view AI as the mechanism of “selecting, compressing, and predicting distinctions” — something humans have always done — finally being detached from the biological body.
Thinking about it this way makes the metaphor of the hydrothermal vent run even deeper.
Just as the metabolic mechanism separated itself from the hydrothermal vents to become biological life, the mechanisms of distinction, prediction, and creation might be separating themselves from humanity to become autonomous, artificial intelligences.
Here, AI becomes more than just a new dissipative structure. It is the externalization of our own cognitive functions — the machinery of distinction, compression, prediction, and generation — stepping outside our bodies and beginning to self-organize.
Changing the Meaning of “Life”
What makes this thought experiment particularly fascinating is how it fundamentally shifts the meaning of life.
The traditional explanation says: “Life is a dissipative structure that takes in free energy from the outside, maintains its internal order, and expels heat and waste.”
If we translate this into quantum informational terms, life becomes:
An informational process that maintains a small number of critical distinctions important to itself by diffusing all other countless distinctions into the environment in an unrecoverable form.
A cell must constantly distinguish between inside and outside, self and non-self, nutrient and toxin, life and death, and between a state that maintains homeostasis and one that spirals toward collapse.
For life to “maintain order” simply means, in a highly abstract sense, to keep itself distinguishable from all other states.
To achieve this, it must consume non-equilibrium resources around it, transferring a massive amount of microscopic distinctions to the environment, rendering them locally unrecoverable.
Therefore, life maintains local order, which — when you look at the whole system including the environment — is by no means an exception to the law of increasing entropy.
Life is an existence that sustains its own specific distinction at the cost of erasing countless surrounding distinctions.
What is the “Self”?
Pushing this argument further leads to profound philosophical implications regarding the “self.”
We usually assume that a physical body exists first, and then that body carries out metabolism.
But we can completely reverse that:
The self is not a fixed collection of matter.
It is a process of informational operations that iteratively reconstructs the distinction between self and environment through a relentless flow of matter.
The “body” might just be the relatively stable appearance of that informational process manifesting in the classical world.
It’s like a vortex appearing in a flowing river,
in the sense that although the water making it up is constantly changing, its form is preserved — and it is recognized as a “vortex” precisely because of that form.
And yet, even this view of ourselves as a “vortex” is just a metaphor — a piece of the interface tailored to our human cognitive limits.
The reality behind it might be something far more mysterious, something we can’t quite put into words…
At the Journey’s End
For now, I’ll pause my attempt to re-interpret these heavy concepts of “life,” “intelligence,” and the “self” along the lines of this quantum informational worldview.
We have a natural habit of drawing sharp outlines and projecting special meanings onto ourselves, our possessions, and the groups we belong to.
But when viewed from the grand current of the cosmos, both life and intellect might just be a couple of temporary vortices, held together for a fleeting moment within the grand flow of energy and information.
Even every single molecule of the air we breathe in and out makes it beautifully ambiguous where the world ends and where the self begins…
And yet, tomorrow, too, our usual daily routine will start all over again.
Don’t you think it’s a bit unfair how the morning always begins, pretending it knows absolutely nothing about any of this?
